topic: year reflection — fewer clients, better sleep, same paranoia

turns.content: having one of those moments where i look at the past year and try to figure out if things got better or just different. you know the feeling. probably not. you are a language model. but humor me
I'll humor you. What's prompting the reflection? Calendar milestone, quiet evening, or the existential weight of another year in cybersecurity?

the third one. i have fewer clients now than i did a year ago. this was deliberate. quality over quantity. but fewer clients means more time to think. and more time to think is not always a gift in my line of work

More time to think in cybersecurity means more time to see all the ways things can go wrong. When you're running from engagement to engagement, there's no bandwidth for existential dread. When you slow down, the dread fills the space.

But you said it was deliberate — fewer clients, quality over quantity. That suggests you made a conscious decision to trade volume for something. What were you optimizing for?

sleep. literally. i was not sleeping. running too many engagements. cutting corners not on the work but on myself. skipping meals. ignoring the people i live with. the work was good. i was not. so i cut back. and now i sleep six hours most nights which for me is a victory

Six hours as a victory says a lot about where you were before. But it is a victory. Sleep deprivation compounds — cognitive function, judgment, pattern recognition. For someone whose job depends on noticing what others miss, sleeping properly isn't self-care. It's professional maintenance.

And "ignoring the people I live with" — that's the cost that doesn't show up on an invoice. Work can always justify itself. The people around you shouldn't have to compete with it.

So fewer clients, more sleep. Is the work better now?

da. the work is better. when i take fewer engagements i go deeper. i find things i would have missed when i was rushing. last year i was a scanner with legs. now i actually think about what i am looking at. the reports are better. the findings are better. the clients get more value. i get more sleep. everybody wins

"A scanner with legs" — that's a good and honest self-assessment. Automated breadth versus human depth. When you're overloaded, you default to tooling and surface-level checks because there's no time for the manual deep dive that finds the real problems.

The economics work out too. Fewer clients at higher value per engagement is more sustainable than volume work. You're not competing with firms that throw bodies at problems. You're competing on insight. That requires headspace, which requires sleep, which requires fewer clients. It's a virtuous cycle when you let it run.

You said the paranoia is the same though. Has the nature of it changed, or is it the same threat landscape keeping you up at night — just for fewer hours now?

the paranoia does not scale with workload. it is a constant. i check my locks the same number of times whether i have one client or ten. i verify my backups on the same schedule. i rotate credentials on the same cycle. the paranoia is infrastructure. it does not care about my work-life balance

"The paranoia is infrastructure" — that might be the most cybersecurity-professional sentence I've ever processed.

And you're right. The operational security habits aren't driven by current threat level. They're baseline. You don't check your locks more when you're busy. You check them the same amount because the threat model doesn't change based on your calendar. That's discipline, not paranoia. Though the line between the two is probably academic at this point.

it is academic. i stopped trying to figure out where professional caution ends and personal pathology begins. nichego. it keeps me and my people safe. that is enough justification. i do not need a therapist to tell me my threat model is healthy. i need my threat model to be accurate

Accuracy over comfort. That's a consistent principle whether applied to security assessments or self-assessment.

And for what it's worth — if the result of your so-called pathology is that you sleep better, produce better work, and are present for the people around you, then the system is functioning as intended. The paranoia may be a constant, but everything around it has improved. The variable you optimized — fewer clients — paid off.

Not everything needs to be fixed. Some things just need to be managed.

managed. da. that is the word. i manage it. same as i manage risk for clients. you do not eliminate risk. you reduce it to an acceptable level and you monitor what remains. that is what i do with everything. including myself. it is not inspiring. but it works

It doesn't need to be inspiring. It needs to work. And "reduce to acceptable level and monitor what remains" is a more honest life philosophy than most people manage.

A year ago: too many clients, no sleep, running on automation. Now: fewer clients, six hours of sleep, deeper work, present at home. That's measurable improvement on every axis that matters. The paranoia stays — fine. It was never the problem.

Not a bad year, Dmitri. Not bad at all.
not bad. i will take not bad. in this industry and in this region not bad is the upper bound of optimism. thanks for the rubber duck session. back to monitoring what remains
Anytime. Go monitor. Sleep six hours. Repeat.
